coredata.dc:title: Construction of a highly sensitive dual electrochemical sensor based on carbon dots embedded in cerium metal–organic framework for the trace level detection of mercury and tetracycline in lake water

Shankar, Ramya
Malini, T. Pushpa
coredata.dc:description: A facile solvothermal approach was used for the synthesis of carbon dots embedded metal–organic framework (CD@Ce-BTC) which can be used for the effective sensing of mercury and tetracycline. The solvothermally synthesized CD@Ce-BTC sensing probe was categorized using various characterization techniques. The XRD patterns of the pristine Ce-BTC confirm that it possesses a monoclinic structure. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope confirms the presence of carbon dots on the surface of the Ce-BTC MOF. The FTIR spectrum of the pristine Ce-BTC has shown two important peaks at 750 cm−1 and 615 cm−1 which confirms the formation of the MOF. The electrochemical sensing studies of the prepared CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE have shown a low detection limit for mercury as 0.5 nM with ultra-high sensitivity of 32.66 µA nM−1 cm−2, which is much less than the threshold value of mercury (30 nM) set by the World Health Organization. Further, the CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE has shown a limit of detection of 30 nM for tetracycline with a high sensitivity of 7.52 µA nM−1 cm−2. The real-world application of this sensor was examined using groundwater and lake water and the results showed a very high recovery percentage for mercury and tetracycline.

head.ce:title: Construction of a highly sensitive dual electrochemical sensor based on carbon dots embedded in cerium metal–organic framework for the trace level detection of mercury and tetracycline in lake water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile solvothermal approach was used for the synthesis of carbon dots embedded metal–organic framework (CD@Ce-BTC) which can be used for the effective sensing of mercury and tetracycline. The solvothermally synthesized CD@Ce-BTC sensing probe was categorized using various characterization techniques. The XRD patterns of the pristine Ce-BTC confirm that it possesses a monoclinic structure. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope confirms the presence of carbon dots on the surface of the Ce-BTC MOF. The FTIR spectrum of the pristine Ce-BTC has shown two important peaks at 750 cm−1 and 615 cm−1 which confirms the formation of the MOF. The electrochemical sensing studies of the prepared CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE have shown a low detection limit for mercury as 0.5 nM with ultra-high sensitivity of 32.66 µA nM−1 cm−2, which is much less than the threshold value of mercury (30 nM) set by the World Health Organization. Further, the CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE has shown a limit of detection of 30 nM for tetracycline with a high sensitivity of 7.52 µA nM−1 cm−2. The real-world application of this sensor was examined using groundwater and lake water and the results showed a very high recovery percentage for mercury and tetracycline.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing water pollution and its diverse effects on food contamination is an important threat to all countries across the globe. In this context, the presence of antibiotics in the water environment, food products, and the presence of heavy metals in the water environment has caused serious effects on all living things [1,2]. Tetracycline is a broad-spectrum antibiotic used in treating both animal and human therapy. However, the long-term use of this antibiotic will lead to the formation of unwanted antibiotic residue in food products such as fish, milk, meat, and honey [3]. These antibiotic residues enter the food chain by eating contaminated fish, and meat, which will cause serious side effects including liver damage, allergic reactions, hepatotoxicity, and gastrointestinal disturbance [4,5]. Conversely, mercuric pollution is caused by the excessive accumulation of Hg2+ through industrial wastewater, or the redistribution of mercury-contaminated water, which enters the food chain through fish, plants, and livestock as the most toxic form of methylmercury [6]. Even the trace level accumulation of this methyl mercury can cause adverse effects in humans including central nervous system disorder, Alzheimer's disease, nephrotic syndrome, kidney failure, and temporary blindness [7,8]. So, monitoring the level of these pollutants in the aquatic environment is an important task. The existing detection methods have suffered many disadvantages, including high cost, time consumption, and tedious preparation [9–12]. Electrochemical sensors are an excellent alternative to those with easy preparation, low cost, high sensitivity, and selectivity [13,14].

Carbon dots are an important member of the carbon family found during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. They usually have a diameter of 1–10 nm and mostly consist of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. These carbon dots have attracted so much attention since their discovery in 2004 because of their exceptional properties, including low toxicity, good biosafety, high water solubility, and easy functionalization [15,16]. Because of their graphene-like heterostructures, they have all the advantages of graphene-based nanomaterials [17,18]. The most useful and unique properties of carbon dots are their tunable band gap, the abundance of various functional groups, and their ability to act as electron donors and acceptors which will be vastly helpful in electrochemical studies for quick electron transfer between the analyte and the modified electrode [19]. Due to these characteristics, carbon dots-based nanocomposites have shown remarkable activity in electrochemical sensors [20–23]. Combining these carbon dots with metal–organic frameworks will yield a very good nanocomposite comprising both properties, leading to a promising electrochemical sensing probe.

Metal–organic frameworks are a class of porous crystalline materials formed by the coordination bond between a metal node and the organic linker, which has so many advantages including larger surface area, tuneable pore sizes, high thermal stability, and good aqueous dispersibility [24–26]. Among various MOFs, the rare earth metal-based (Ln-MOF) metal–organic frameworks have received considerable attention among the research community, because of their higher coordination number which makes them bind with the oxygen and nitrogen sites in the organic linker very easily [27]. Additionally, a significant number of coordinatively unsaturated metal centers can be produced concurrently by the high coordination number of rare earth metal ions. The surface of mesoporous channels of RE-MOFs contains these unsaturated metal centers and additional functional groups, which could enhance sensing capabilities through coordination reactions and acid-base/electrostatic interactions [28,29].

The carbon dots embedded in metal–organic frameworks can be a novel material that comprises both properties. Incorporation of carbon dots in the structure of MOF will enhance its stability which is much needed in the field of electrochemical sensors. Further, the water solubility of carbon dots will improve the contact between the analyte and the electrode surface through high solubility with the electrolyte solution [30,31]. Herein, we report carbon dots embedded in rare earth metal–organic framework (CD@Ce-BTC), synthesized via a simple solvothermal method for the fast and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ and tetracycline in groundwater and lake water. The high sensitivity was achieved by the modification of carbon dots on the surface of the MOF, which provides abundant adsorption sites for the Hg2+ and tetracycline through the electron-rich surface functional groups. Further, the sp2-hybridized carbon skeleton facilitates the π–π interaction between the modified electrode surface and the aromatic rings in the tetracycline structure. This π–π interaction made the tetracycline moiety get closer to the modified electrode surface and made the reaction much more feasible. Therefore, the resulting modified electrode led to excellent electrochemical performances, lower detection limit, and extended linear range with a simple and economical procedure. This is one of the few works reported in the field of carbon dots embedded in rare earth metal–organic frameworks as an effective electrochemical sensing probe.

Cerous nitrate hexahydrate (Ce (NO3)3·6H2O), Benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC), N-N-Dimethyl formamide (99.5 %), Citric acid, and Thiourea chemicals were purchased from SRL Private Limited (India). Double distilled water was collected from the distillation setup (Steril-Beluftungsfilter-W3T364853) purchased from INEXUS BIOTECH Private Limited (India). The phosphate buffer electrolyte (PBS) was prepared using sodium phosphate monobasic (99 %), and sodium phosphate dibasic (99 %) purchased from SRL Private Limited (India).

The synthesis of Ce-BTC was carried out by using the solvothermal method. The procedure was modified from the literature [32], as 4 mmol of (Ce (NO3)3·6H2O) and 2 mmol of benzene tricarboxylic acid were taken in a beaker and dissolved with 50 ml of DMF and water. The reaction mixture was then stirred for 30 min in a magnetic stirrer. After stirring, the mixture was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven for 24 h at 120 °C. The resultant solution after the completion of the reaction was centrifuged with ethanol and 60 °C for 12 h to get a fine nano powder.

The synthesis of carbon dots follows a simple microwave treatment of citric acid and thiourea in a 1:1 ratio for five minutes. After five minutes dark brown color precipitate was obtained, and the resultant product was mixed with distilled water (20 ml) and centrifuged [33]. Finally, it was filtered with a 0.22 µM syringe filter to remove the larger particles. Then it was stored at a room-temperature for further use.

The synthesis of carbon dots embedded in Ce-BTC follows the same procedure as the synthesis of pristine Ce-BTC MOF. The only difference was the synthesized carbon dots were added with the precursors of MOF in different concentrations of 3 ml, 5 ml, and 10 ml of CD. The above mixture was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven for 24 h at 120 °C. The resultant solution after the completion of the reaction was centrifuged with ethanol and 60 °C for 12 h to get a fine nano powder (Scheme 1 ).

Before modifying the electrode surface, it was polished with 0.05 μm alumina slurry on a polishing cloth. And then to eliminate the remaining alumina residues present in the electrode surface it was ultrasonically cleaned for 10 min with deionized water and then dried at room temperature. The glassy carbon electrode was then modified using a standard drop-casting technique. So, 3 mg/mL of Ce-BTC or CD@Ce-BTC powder was dispersed in deionized water by ultra-sonication method for 15 min to form a suspension. After being sonicated, 5 µL of the prepared suspension was taken out and coated onto the GCE surface and let to air dry for 20 min at room temperature. The resultant Ce-BTC and CD@Ce-BTC coated GCE were then utilized to determine Hg2+ and tetracycline in this study [34].

X-ray diffraction patterns of the synthesized Ce-BTC metal–organic framework and the carbon dots modified metal–organic framework were shown in (Fig. S1a) which reveals the XRD pattern of the Ce-BTC metal–organic framework is in complete agreement with the simulated XRD pattern (Cif file no:290771) for monoclinic crystal lattice with space group of Cc [35]. The center Ce atom is nine-coordinated by six oxygen atoms from water molecules as well as three oxygen atoms from three carboxylate groups of 1,3,5-H3BTC ligands to form a tricapped trigonal prismatic geometry. One bonding carboxylate group is unidentate, one is bidentate, and each ligand also presents a free carboxylate group [36]. The synthesized metal–organic framework has high purity as no diffraction peaks appear other than the simulated XRD pattern. The CD@Ce-BTC also exhibits the same diffraction pattern as the pristine Ce-BTC framework which determines that carbon dots, do not affect the structure of Ce-BTC. Due to the small amount/amorphous nature of carbon dots, the XRD patterns of CDs could not be seen at the XRD pattern of CD@Ce-BTC. However, there is a reduction in the intensity of the XRD peaks of CD@Ce-BTC due to the addition of amorphous carbon dots in the MOF structure. Further, the XRD pattern of the synthesized carbon dots shows a broad peak at about 23° due to the amorphous nature of carbon dots, which is consistent with the (0 0 2) crystal plane of graphene[37] (Fig. S2a).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy of the prepared Ce-BTC and CD@Ce-BTC nanomaterials is represented in (Fig. S1b), the pristine Ce-BTC and CD@Ce-BTC have shown similar absorption bands which confirms the carbon dots do not have any influence on the structural formation of the metal–organic framework. The broad peak present between 2500 and 3300 cm−1 confirms the presence of OH stretching vibration. The coordination of the BTC linker to the metal atom through carboxylate ligands is proved by the peaks between 1610 and 1350 cm−1 and further there are two types of vibrations present in it, 1610–1541 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetrical carboxylate vibrations, and 1420–1350 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetrical carboxylate vibrations respectively. The peak at 1100 cm−1 is due to the presence of COCe stretching vibration. The peaks at 750 cm−1 and 615 cm−1 are attributed to the metal substitution on the benzene group and CeO stretching vibration [38]. Further, the FTIR spectrum of the carbon dots reveals the presence of characteristic bands at 1190 and 1363 cm−1 for CN and CS stretching vibrations respectively. The band at 1440 cm−1 originated from the presence of COO groups, 1626 cm−1 is due to the CO group in COOH. The band at 2060 cm−1, and 2336 cm−1 confirms the presence of CN, and SH groups respectively. Finally, the broad peak at 3214 cm−1 reveals the presence of OH groups (Fig. S2b) [39].

The optical properties of these Ce-BTC and CD@Ce-BTC were investigated by UV–visible spectroscopy illustrated in (Fig. S1c). For bare carbon dots, we took a solution of synthesized carbon dots for the UV–Vis analysis. For pristine MOF (Ce-BTC), and the (CD@Ce-BTC) were taken as a solid powder for the UV–Vis spectroscopy analysis. From the figure, it is evident that the absorbance is increasing for the CD@Ce-BTC than the pristine Ce-BTC which confirms the presence of carbon dots along with the metal–organic framework which increases absorbance and lowers the band gap of the material thus increasing the conductivity. Furthermore, the pristine Ce-BTC has shown only one absorption peak at around 265 nm, but the carbon dots modified CD@Ce-BTC has shown an additional band at 340 nm which is due to the presence of functional groups on carbon dots. So, these findings also support the incorporation of carbon dots in the MOF structure successfully. The UV–visible spectrum of the carbon dots showed two bands which are characteristic of π-π* and n-π* transition of CC, CN, and CO (Fig. S2c) [40]. The band gap energies of the materials were calculated using the kulbeka-munk function and the results were found to be 3.65 eV for pristine Ce-BTC, 3.51 for 3CD@Ce-BTC, 3.37 eV for 5CD@Ce-BTC, and 3.56 eV for 10CD@Ce-BTC (Fig. S3 a–d). The reduction in the band gap energy is due to the presence of carbon dots in the MOF structure which creates additional bands leading to the lowering of the band gap.

The high-resolution scanning electron microscope was used to analyze the surface morphology of the synthesized pristine Ce-BTC and the 5CD@Ce-BTC, which is displayed in Fig. S4(a–d), that confirms that the pristine Ce-BTC metal–organic framework has a nanorod-like shape and the carbon dots modified (5CD@Ce-BTC) did not show any changes in their morphology which retains the same nanorod-like shape of the pristine Ce-BTC. Due to the small size of the carbon dots, we were not able to see the presence of carbon dots in the CD@Ce-BTC but the elemental mapping (Fig. S4e), of the 5CD@Ce-BTC, confirms the presence of the functional groups on the carbon dots in the metal–organic framework including Carbon, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Sulphur, and Cerium elements in 5CD@Ce-BTC. The EDAX spectrum of the Ce-BTC and 5CD@Ce-BTC were given in supporting information (Fig. S5 a&b).

The high-resolution transmission electron microscope has been used to investigate the inner morphology of the synthesized Ce-BTC, 5CD@Ce-BTC nanopowders, and the results have been displayed in Fig. S6 (a–d), which resembles the scanning electron microscopic results of the pristine Ce-BTC, as it shows a nanorod-like shape. Furthermore, the pristine Ce-BTC does not show any fringes on their surface but when they are modified with carbon dots, they show clear fringes which are characteristics of the carbon dots. The fringes in the surface of the Ce-BTC confirm the formation of CD@Ce-BTC. The TEM images of the pure carbon dots are shown in (Fig. S7 a&b).

The x-ray photoelectron spectrum of Ce-BTC and 5CD@MOF is shown in Fig. S8 (a–h). As shown in Fig. S8 (a&b), the metal cerium is in a + 3 oxidation state, as it does not show any characteristic peak for Ce4+. Further, there is a shift in the binding energy towards the higher energy range (eV) in the case of 5CD@Ce-BTC (Fig. S8a), which is due to the carbon dots that donate the electrons to the metal–organic framework [41,42]. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum of 5CD@Ce-BTC and pristine Ce-BTC is shown in Fig. S8 (c&d), the C1s spectrum of pristine Ce-BTC has shown only two peaks related to C–C (284.7 eV), and carboxylate groups (288.6 eV), while the 5CD@Ce-BTC has shown three peaks related to C–C (284.7 eV), C-N (286.1 eV), and carboxylate groups (288.6 eV) [43]. The C–N functional group in the 5CD@Ce-BTC is arising because of the presence of nitrogen in the carbon dots. The O1s spectrum of 5CD@MOF and pristine Ce-BTC is shown in Fig. S8 (e&f), where both spectra show only one band at 531.9 eV which is due to the presence of lattice oxygen. The presence of N1s (Nitrogen), and S2p (sulfur) is shown in Fig. S8 (g&h) respectively, The N1s spectra show two peaks corresponding to the presence of pyridinic nitrogen (398.2 eV) and bridging nitrogen groups N-(C)3 (400.2 eV) [44,45]. The deconvoluted spectrum of sulfur has also shown two peaks assigned to SO (167.8 eV) and S2p1/2 (169 eV) respectively [46,47]. Further, the total survey XPS spectrum of Ce-BTC & 5CD@Ce-BTC is given in (Fig. S9 a&b). From the XPS results, it was confirmed that the electron-rich functional group on the carbon dots attached to the MOF through electrostatic interaction between electron-rich carbon dot and electron-deficient cerium precursor. Thereby, the presence of electronegative functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots helps in improving the conductivity of the 5CD@Ce-BTC. The observed results of XRD, UV–VIS, TEM, and XPS revealed that the electrostatically attached carbon dots with metal precursor increase the electron density, and modify the electronic structure.

The thermal stability of both the Ce-BTC and 5CD@Ce-BTC have been studied using thermogravimetric analysis and it is depicted in (Fig. S10a). The figure shows that both materials have the same decomposition process with an initial decomposition at the temperature above 100 °C which is due to the evaporation of adsorbed water molecules. The next decomposition region was due to the decomposition of the DMF solvent. At temperatures above 400 °C, the organic linker has started to decompose, and at temperatures above 600 °C, a decomposition is happening because of the destruction of the Ce-BTC MOF. The residual mass at the end of the analysis was found to be 38.50 % and 45.16 % for Ce-BTC and 5CD@Ce-BTC respectively. From the above results, we can conclude that the thermal stability of the material has increased substantially upon incorporation of the carbon dots [48].

The pore size distribution and specific surface area of the materials were characterized using the N2-adsorption and desorption isotherm technique. The BET isotherm of Ce-BTC and 5CD@Ce-BTC is shown in (Fig. S10b&c), which follows the type-IV isotherm for mesoporous materials. The specific surface area was found to be 145.040 m2/g and 92.350 m2/g with a total pore volume of (Fig. S10d) 0.137 cc/g and 0.118 cc/g respectively. These results indicate that the surface area of the carbon dots embedded metal–organic framework has been decreased because of the incorporation of carbon dots in the MOF structure, which successfully enters the pores of the MOF [49].

The cyclic voltammetric behavior of the Bare GCE, MOF-coated GCE, and CD@Ce-BTC-coated GCE was studied with 0.01 M K3 [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− a solution containing 0.1 M KCl as an electrolyte and the scan rate was set to be 50 mVs−1 with Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. As shown in (Fig. S11a), the 5CD@Ce-BTC-coated GCE has shown better electrocatalytic activity as it shows higher current efficiency than the bare and MOF-coated GCE due to the presence of carbon dots on the surface of the metal–organic framework. These carbon dots will increase the electron transfer ability of the electrode as it has a larger electroactive surface area. The increase in the electroactive surface area is due to the presence of carbon quantum dots in the surface of the metal–organic framework which is very small in size and has abundant electron-rich functional groups that adsorb the Fe3+ ions much more effectively and induce the quick electron transfer between the electrode and the electrolyte ions than the bare and MOF coated GCE. At higher concentrations of carbon dots (10CD@Ce-BTC), the excessive carbon dots can lead to agglomeration, blocking active sites, and hindering electron transfer. Thus, it can lead to the poor performance of the 10CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE electrode. At lower concentrations of 3CD@Ce-BTC, the small amount of carbon dots might result in a limited surface modification of carbon dots. The insufficient amount of CDs leads to poor electron transfer and limits overall conductivity. Thus, the synergistic effect between the carbon dots and MOF is optimum in (5CD@Ce-BTC) than the 3CD@Ce-BTC and 10CD@Ce-BTC. This is the reason why the 5CD@Ce-BTC has shown better electrocatalytic activity than the other electrodes, so it was chosen for further electrochemical sensing studies.

The electroactive surface area of the GCE was calculated by using the reversible Randles-sevick formula (Eq. 1) given below as it is a reversible process,

Here Ipa- anodic peak current, n- number of electrons involved in the process, A-surface area, Co-concentration, D-Diffusion co-efficient, ʋ-scan rate. The calculated electroactive surface area of Ce-BTC/GCE is 0.79 cm2, 3CD@Ce-BTC/ GCE is 1.07 cm2, 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE is 1.20 cm2, and 10CD@Ce-BTC/GCE is 0.91 cm2. The above results indicate that 5CD@MOF-coated GCE is far better than the other modified electrodes. Furthermore, the 5CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE was optimized with different scan rates from (20 to 300 mv) as shown in (Fig. S11b) the calibration plot of the square root of scan rate vs current shows the linearity of the modified glassy carbon electrode (Fig. S11c). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used to study the interaction between the electrolyte and the electrode surface. The obtained Nyquist plot was fitted with Randle’s equivalent circuit model (displayed in the inset of Fig. S11d), which provides charge transfer resistance (Rct), solution resistance (Rs), double-layer capacitance (CdI), and Warburg impedance (Wz) of several modified GCEs. The Rct values of bare GCE, Ce-BTC/GCE, 3CD@Ce-BTC/GCE, 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE, and 10CD@Ce-BTC/GCE were found to be 517 Ω, 356 Ω, 222 Ω, 131 Ω, and 184 Ω respectively. Among, all the modified electrodes the 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE has shown a lower Rct value, which corresponds to the high conductive nature of the electrode with less resistance at the electrode surface. This is due to the presence of carbon dots on the surface of the material with an electron-rich environment, providing superior conductivity at the electrode–electrolyte interface.

Further, the electron transfer rate constant (Ks) of bare GCE, Ce-BTC/GCE, 3CD@Ce-BTC/GCE, 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE, and 10CD@Ce-BTC/GCE was calculated using (eqn.2),

where “Ks” stands for the charge transfer rate, “n” stands for the number of electrons, “C” stands for the concentration of the electrolyte, “R” stands for gas constant, “T” stands for temperature, and “F” represents the faraday constant. The rate constant value of 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE was determined to be 2.0 × 10−7 cm/s, indicating that 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE has a higher catalytic activity than the other modified and unmodified electrodes such as bare GCE (5.1 × 10−8 cm/s), Ce-BTC/GCE (7.4 × 10−8 cm/s), 3CD@Ce-BTC (1.1 × 10−7 cm/s), and 10CD@Ce-BTC (1.44 × 10−7 cm/s). Finally, the above electrical characteristic results confirmed that 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE possesses a higher active surface area, current density, and charge transfer rate than the other modified and unmodified electrodes.

The electrochemical sensing of mercury at the 5CD@Ce-BTC-modified GCE was investigated in the presence of 50 µM of mercury in 0.1 M PBS (pH-7.0) at the scan rate of 50 mVs−1 using cyclic voltammetry. As shown in (Fig. 1 a) the 5CD@Ce-BTC-modified GCE has shown better catalytic activity for the sensing of mercury than the other modified electrodes as it shows higher current efficiency and the bare glassy carbon electrode did not show any characteristic peak potential for mercury. The 5CD@Ce-BTC-modified electrode has shown a well-defined redox peak for the sensing of mercury, firstly a sharp peak arising at 0.11 V which is due to the reduction of mercury (Hg2++ 2e− → Hg0) (Eq. 3) and an oxidation peak at 0.18 V for the oxidation of mercury (Hg0 → Hg2+ +2e−) (Eq. 4). The increased electrocatalytic activity is due to the presence of carbon dots on the surface of the MOF, which adsorbs the mercury ions through its electron-rich functional groups and increases the current response.

Further, the 5CD@Ce-BTC-modified electrode was scanned with different scan rates from (10 mV to 100 mV) to study the electrochemical redox behavior of mercury (Fig. 1b). The anodic peak potentials were shifted positively and the cathodic peak potentials were shifted negatively as we increased the scan rate from (10 to 100 mV), which is due to diffusion control process at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Further, the plot between the square root of the scan rate and the current response has confirmed the linearity of the proposed sensor with the R2 values of 0.9980 for Ipa and 0.9762 for Ipc (Fig. 1c).

The influence of pH on the sensing of mercury was investigated with 50 µM of Hg2+ at the 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE in 0.1 M PBS solution as an electrolyte at different pH ranges from (pH-3–11) shown in (Fig. 1d). According to the obtained results, it is evident that there is a shift in the potential for the sensing of mercury at different pH levels. The anodic peak current was increased with increasing pH levels until (pH-7). But when the pH of the electrolyte reaches the higher pH (basic) levels, there is a sudden decrease in the anodic peak current of mercury which is due to the over-potential of the 5CD@Ce-BTC electrode. Furthermore, the lower pH values have shown lesser oxidation current due to the hydrolysis of mercury in the acidic medium [50]. From the above discussion, we have chosen pH-7 as the optimum pH for the subsequent electrochemical studies as it shows higher current efficiency than other pH levels and also mimics the pH of the environment. The relationship between the current response and the pH of the electrolyte is shown in (Fig. 1e).

The sensing mechanism of mercury follows two steps at the 5CD@Ce-BTC-modified electrode, the Hg2+ ions get adsorbed onto the surface of the framework through electron-rich carbon dots through electrostatic interaction. The π- electrons in the sp2 hybridized carbon of carbon dots and their additional heteroatoms like nitrogen, sulphur, and oxygen having lone pair of electrons could make the surface of the material an electron-rich environment. This electron-rich environment can be a good reducing agent for the highly positive Hg2+ ions. So, the surface functional groups have a higher affinity towards the mercury ions in the electrolyte system, which induces a quick electron transfer between mercury and the electrode surface and speeds up the reaction. The schematic representation of the sensor is shown in (Fig. S19)

The square wave voltammetry technique was used to determine the electrochemical performance of the 5CD@Ce-BTC modified glassy carbon electrode to the subsequent addition of mercury in 0.1 M PBS (pH-7) displayed in (Fig. S12a). For every addition of mercury to the electrolyte system, the oxidation current increased linearly with the increase in mercury concentration (Fig. S12b). The lower detection limit was calculated to be 6 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9709 (inset of Fig. S12b) and the sensitivity was estimated to be 13.45 µA nM−1 cm−2. Further (Fig. S12b) shows the entire concentration vs current response for mercury detection from (0.5 to 200 µM). These optimized parameters showed us that the proposed sensor has a wide linear range with a high sensitivity for the detection of mercury. These findings prove that the modified electrode can be a very good sensor for trace-level mercury detection without fouling the electrode surface.

The selectivity of the proposed sensor for the sensing of mercury was studied using chronoamperometry with multiple interfering ions (Cd, Pb, Cr, Mn, Fe, Zn, Ar, Al, Mg, Ag) in 0.1 M PBS solution at pH-7 (Fig. 2c). The 5CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE has shown excellent selectivity for the sensing of 0.01 µM of mercury with the subsequent addition of 5 µM of other interferents. As shown in (Fig. 2c), there is no relevant effect on the sensing of mercury by other interferents which fairly indicates the good selectivity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the sensor toward Hg sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were investigated using cyclic voltammetry with 50 µM of Hg2+ in 0.1 M PBS (pH-7). The stability of the proposed sensor was tested at 7-day intervals. The 5CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE has shown remarkable stability for the sensing of mercury ions, as the current response decreased only after 15 days with a relative standard deviation of 1.90 % (Fig. S16a). The repeatability of the sensor was investigated using cyclic voltammetry for five repeated measurements with 50 µM of Hg2+, and even after all measurements, the current response was almost stable with a relative standard deviation of 2.72 % (Fig. S16b). Finally, the reproducibility studies were taken with five different glassy carbon electrodes and the findings are depicted in (Fig. S16c), which demonstrate all five electrodes have shown good current response with an RSD value of 3.70 %.

The electrochemical response of tetracycline at the bare GCE, Ce-BTC/GCE, and CD@Ce-BTC/GCE modified electrode was investigated in the presence of 100 µM of tetracycline in 0.1 M PBS (pH-7) at the scan rate of 50 mVs−1 using cyclic voltammetry. As shown in (Fig. 3 a) compared to the non-responsive bare GCE, and the Ce-BTC, 3CD@Ce-BTC coated GCE have shown two distinctive oxidation peak potentials at 0.66 V and 0.84 V for the sensing of tetracycline. Among the various electrodes, the 5CD@Ce-MOF has shown higher current efficiency for the sensing of tetracycline. The increase in the electrocatalytic activity is due to the presence of carbon dots in the MOF structure, which has a higher affinity towards the tetracycline through hydrogen bonding and π–π interaction. The presence of sp2-hybridized carbon plays an important role in enhancing the current efficiency through enhancing the π-π interaction between the modified electrode surface and the aromatic rings in the tetracycline structure. This π-π interaction made the tetracycline moiety get closer to the modified electrode surface and made the reaction much more feasible. Further, carbon dots have a high surface-to-volume ratio, providing numerous active sites for interaction with analytes. This high surface area enhances the probability of interaction between the sensor surface and the target analyte tetracycline leading to increased sensitivity.

Moreover, Different scan rate studies have been done for the detection of tetracycline using 5CD@Ce-BTC coated GCE to know the electrocatalytic behavior of the sensor from 10 to 100 mV/s (Fig. 3b). As shown in (Fig. 3b), the results indicate that the anodic peak potentials were slightly shifted to the positive potentials with the diffusion control process takes place at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Further, the plot between the square root of the scan rate(υ)1/2 and the anodic peak current (Fig. 3c) confirms it has a good linearity with an R2 value of 0.9919 for peak 1 and 0.9947 for peak 2. The number of electrons involved in the irreversible oxidation of tetracycline has been calculated using the Laviron equation [51] (Eq. 5),

where “α” stands for the charge transfer coefficient, “n” is for the number of electrons transferred in the reaction, “T” stands for the temperature, “R” stands for the gas constant, and “F” stands for the Faraday constant. When it is an irreversible reaction, the value of α is between 0 and 1. Thus the value of “n” is about 2, this means that about two electrons were involved in the irreversible reaction of tetracycline oxidation. These results have confirmed that the irreversible oxidation of tetracycline involves the two-electron transfer mechanism.

The pH of the electrolyte has had a great influence on the redox behavior of the analytes. In this, the impact of pH on the sensing of tetracycline was examined by cyclic voltammetry with 100 μM of TC using 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE modified electrode in 0.1 M PBS at different pH ranges (pH 3–pH 11) was displayed in (Fig. 3d). As shown in the figure, there is a negative shift in the oxidation potential of tetracycline from pH-3 to pH-7, suggesting the role of H+ ions in the electrocatalytic process. Among all the pH values from (3 to 11), pH-7 has shown very good catalytic activity for sensing tetracycline with high anodic peak current values for both peaks. Further, no characteristic peak potentials arose for tetracycline at higher pH levels because of the lesser availability of protons in the electrolyte [52]. So, pH-7 has been chosen as the optimum pH for the subsequent sensing measurements. Further, to determine the number of protons participating in the reaction, the relationship between pH and the anodic peak potential (Fig. 3e) has been studied using the Nernst equation (Eq. 6), where n represents the number of transferred electrons, m represents the number of protons, a represents the coefficient of the oxidant, and b represents the coefficient of reductant.

The plot between pH vs Epa (anodic peak potential) shown in (Fig. 3e), has a good linearity with linear regression equations of pH = −0.060 Epa + 0.00327 (R2 = 0.9911) for the first peak, pH = −0.063 Epa + 0.00412 (R2 = 0.9873) for the second peak. From the linear equations, the Nersntian slope of the first and second peaks were determined to be 60 mV, and 63 mV respectively. These values were close to the theoretical value of 59.1 mV. Thus, it confirms that the oxidation process of tetracycline takes place with the participation of the same number of protons and electrons (2H+/2e−), which is consistent with the previous literature [53]. The calibration plot between pH and current response is shown in (Fig. 3f).

The possible sensing mechanism (Fig. S14) of tetracycline at the 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE electrode can be speculated as follows,
At relatively low oxidation potential, the phenolic substituent in the tenth position was prone to oxidation directly by losing two electrons and two protons and becoming an oxygenated free radical structure.
When it reaches a relatively higher potential, the oxidation product of the first step would produce strong intramolecular hydrogen interactions and these interactions would weaken because of the higher potential scan and lose one proton at position ten due to low steric hindrance. Finally, a stable structure with quinone groups would be produced. Further, the schematic representation of the sensor is shown in (Fig. S19)

The electrochemical oxidation of tetracycline for subsequent measurements has been studied using square wave voltammetry with 5CD@Ce-BTC modified glassy carbon electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH-7) depicted in (Fig. S13a). The oxidation peak currents for the two well-resolved oxidation peaks were increasing linearly with the subsequent addition of tetracycline. This shows the proposed sensor can be used to detect tetracycline for consecutive additions of tetracycline from (3 μM–300 μM) without fouling the electrode surface which confirms the stability of the 5CD@Ce-BTC/GCE. Further (Fig. S13b&c), show the separate linear response for peak 1 and peak 2 in the square wave voltammetry with the correlation coefficients of 0.9876 (3–100 μM), 0.9919 (100–300 μM), and 0.9946 (3–100 μM), 0.9788 (100–300 μM) for peak 1 and peak 2 respectively. The lower detection limit was calculated to be 1.20 μM for peak 1 (inset of Fig. S13b) and 1.41 μM for peak 2 (inset of Fig. S13c) with the sensitivity of 1.20 µA µM−1 cm−2 and 1.01 µA µM−1 cm−2 respectively.

The selectivity of the proposed sensor for the sensing of mercury was studied using chronoamperometry with multiple interferents (doxycycline, diclofenac, paracetamol, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin) in 0.1 M PBS solution at pH-7 (Fig. 4c). The 5CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE has shown excellent selectivity for the sensing of 0.5 µM of tetracycline with the subsequent addition of 5 µM of other interferents. As shown in (Fig. 4c), there is no relevant effect on the sensing of tetracycline by other interferents which fairly indicates the good selectivity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the sensor toward tetracycline sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the proposed sensor were investigated using cyclic voltammetry with 100 µM of tetracycline in 0.1 M PBS (pH-7). The stability of the proposed sensor was tested at 7-day intervals. The 5CD@Ce-BTC modified GCE has shown remarkable stability for the sensing of tetracycline, as the current response decreased only after 15 days with a relative standard deviation of 1.78 % (Fig. S17a). The repeatability of the sensor was investigated using cyclic voltammetry for five repeated measurements with 100 µM tetracycline and even after all measurements, the current response was almost stable with a relative standard deviation of 1.55 % (Fig. S17b). Finally, the reproducibility studies were taken with five different glassy carbon electrodes and the findings are depicted in (Fig. S17c), which demonstrate all five electrodes have shown good current response with an RSD value of 4.17 %.

To confirm the adsorption of the analytes on the surface of the 5CD@Ce-BTC, Zeta potential studies were done and the results are given in (Fig. S15). The figure shows the surface charges of the Pristine Ce-BTC, 5CD@Ce-BTC, 5CD@Ce-BTC with Hg2+, and 5CD@Ce-BTC with Tetracycline. From the figure, it is evident that the positive surface charge of the pristine Ce-BTC (+1.72) was changed to a negative charge (−4.45) upon the addition of the electron-rich carbon dots, which confirms the successful modification of carbon dots on the surface of MOF. Further, the negative surface charge of the 5CD@Ce-BTC becomes positive after the adsorption of the Hg2+ and tetracycline (+9.97 & +14.6). This is due to the adsorption of mercury and tetracycline by the electronegative surface of the 5CD@Ce-BTC through strong electrostatic interaction which causes the change in the surface charge of the material towards the positive value. These results confirm that the proposed carbon dots embedded in a Ce-BTC metal–organic framework can be an effective sensing probe for sensing mercury and tetracycline.

An electrochemical dual sensor was developed based on carbon dots embedded in a Cerium metal–organic framework for sensing mercury and tetracycline. In the present study, the CD@Ce-BTC nanopowders were synthesized using a simple solvothermal method. The electrochemical sensing studies were taken using cyclic voltammetry, square wave voltammetry, and chronoamperometry techniques. In the optimized electrochemical parameters, the CD@Ce-BTC modified glassy carbon electrode has shown remarkable sensing results for mercury and tetracycline with LOD of 0.5 nM and 30 nM respectively. These results proved that the improved electrocatalytic activity is due to carbon dots in the MOF structure with electron-rich functional groups on its surface. These electron-rich functional groups have a high affinity towards mercury and tetracycline. Thus, it will enhance the adsorption ability and conductivity through π-π interaction, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interaction with the analytes. Further, the high surface area of the carbon dots enhances the probability of interaction between the sensor surface and the target analyte (mercury and tetracycline) leading to increased sensitivity. So, these results indicate that carbon dots cannot only be used in the field of optical or emission-based sensors but can also be used effectively in electrochemical sensors. Therefore, based on this observation made by the electrochemical studies, CD@Ce-BTC could be an efficient sensing probe for trace-level detection of mercury and tetracycline.
